target.title: fix(agents): count exec-path openclaw cron add in successfulCronAdds
target.body:
### What

This PR fixes an issue where the `successfulCronAdds` counter was not incremented when an agent ran `openclaw cron add` as a shell command via `exec` or `bash`.

### Why

The existing logic only checked for the structured `cron` tool. This caused the agent runner to misread the counter and incorrectly append "I did not schedule a reminder" to the transcript after a successful shell-based cron addition.

### How

An `else if` block is added to `pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.ts` to inspect `exec`/`bash` results. It uses an anchored regex (`/^\s*openclaw\s+cron\s+add\b/i`) to identify the command and requires a success receipt (`created|scheduled`) in the output before incrementing the counter.

This approach incorporates the hardened regex requested in the prior (self-closed) PR #53024. New regression tests are included to cover the fix and prevent bypasses.

Closes #52972

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Agent runner incorrectly appends "I did not schedule a reminder" after the agent runs `openclaw cron add` via exec/bash shell tool (structured `cron` tool path was unaffected).

**Real environment tested:** OpenClaw development clone, Node 24, Linux (WSL2). Exec/bash tool path exercised via the handler directly.

**Exact steps:**
```
pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts --reporter=verbose
```

**Observed result:** All 24 tests pass. The 4 new exec-path cron commitment tests passed: counter increments on a valid `openclaw cron add` with receipt, and does not increment for bypass attempts, missing creation receipts, or non-openclaw commands.

**What was not tested:** Live OpenClaw server with real cron backend and actual scheduled jobs; agents using non-standard shell wrappers around `openclaw cron add`; exec timeout paths.

target.title: fix(agent-job): surface pending error snapshot when outer timeout fires
target.body:
### What

Fixes a race condition in `waitForAgentJob` that caused pending agent errors to be discarded when the primary timeout fired.

### Why

When an agent job fails, an `error` event schedules the job to finish with an error snapshot after a 15-second grace period. If the outer `timeoutMs` for `waitForAgentJob` fires before this grace period elapses, it calls `finish(null)`, canceling the pending error.

The caller receives a generic timeout message instead of the actual root cause, making debugging difficult.

### How

The outer timeout callback now checks for a pending error via `getPendingAgentRunError()` before resolving with `null`. This function reads from a module-level map that is populated as soon as the `error` event is received, guaranteeing that the snapshot is available if the race occurs.

A new regression test using fake timers (`5s < 15s`) confirms the fix.

Closes #52497

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Race condition in `waitForAgentJob` — outer timeout fires `finish(null)`, discarding the pending error snapshot. Caller gets a generic timeout instead of the actual agent failure reason.

**Real environment tested:** OpenClaw development clone, Node 24, Linux (WSL2). Gateway `waitForAgentJob` path exercised directly with controlled timing.

target.title: fix(vllm): wire configured thinking params
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- vLLM thinking controls can now be model-aware instead of exposing a generic multi-level ladder for models that only support binary thinking.
- Configured Qwen thinking params now reach the model catalog used by `/think` and provider policy resolution, including allowlist-only `agents.defaults.models` entries.
- The vLLM stream wrapper now reads the same configured Qwen thinking format as the UI/session policy path.

Why does this matter now?

- This follows the model-aware thinking PRD in #86099 and fixes the inconsistency where configured vLLM/Qwen params could affect streaming while `/think` still showed unsupported levels.

What is the intended outcome?

- Configured vLLM Qwen models expose persistent binary `off`/`on` thinking choices, and those choices map to the correct vLLM request fields.
- Nemotron 3 vLLM reasoning models keep the existing binary profile behavior.
- Runtime-only model params are not exposed through `models.list` responses.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- Changing non-vLLM provider thinking semantics.
- Changing Pi OpenAI harness behavior beyond passing catalog `params`/`compat` into provider thinking policy context.

What does success look like?

- Provider-declared and allowlist-only model params both resolve into the configured catalog.
- `/think` choices and vLLM stream request patching agree for configured Qwen thinking formats.
- Focused model-selection, thinking-policy, vLLM stream, gateway model-list, docs, diff, and build gates pass.

What should reviewers focus on?

- The catalog merge/synthesis path in `src/agents/model-selection-shared.ts`.
- The vLLM-owned binary thinking profile and stream patching contract in `extensions/vllm/thinking-policy.ts` and `extensions/vllm/stream.ts`.
- The `models.list` params redaction in `src/gateway/server-methods/models.ts`.
- The additive provider thinking context docs in `docs/plugins/sdk-provider-plugins.md` and `docs/plugins/sdk-migration.md`.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #86099

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #72329
Related #79677
Related #82768

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

- Requested by Pearson in the vLLM/OpenClaw thinking-mode exploration and captured in #86099.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: vLLM/Qwen thinking configuration can now drive both `/think` choices and stream request patching through the same configured model params/compat seam.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw CLI built from this PR branch, isolated temp config/state, and live k3s vLLM router at `http://127.0.0.1:31080/v1` with bearer token redacted.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `curl -sS -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' http://127.0.0.1:31080/health`
  - `curl -sS -H 'Authorization: Bearer [redacted]' http://127.0.0.1:31080/v1/models`
  - `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-vllm-think-proof/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-vllm-think-proof/state pnpm openclaw models list --provider vllm --json`
  - `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-vllm-think-proof/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-vllm-think-proof/state pnpm openclaw infer model run --local --model vllm/qwen3-5-122b-a10b-nvfp4 --prompt 'Reply exactly: VLLM_THINK_OK' --thinking off --json`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

  ```text
  $ curl -sS -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' http://127.0.0.1:31080/health
  200

  $ curl -sS -H 'Authorization: Bearer [redacted]' http://127.0.0.1:31080/v1/models
  {"object":"list","data":[{"id":"qwen3-vl-embedding-2b","object":"model","created":1779664611,"owned_by":"vllm","root":null,"parent":null},{"id":"qwen3-5-122b-a10b-nvfp4","object":"model","created":1779664611,"owned_by":"vllm","root":null,"parent":null}]}

  $ OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-vllm-think-proof/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-vllm-think-proof/state pnpm openclaw models list --provider vllm --json
  {
    "count": 1,
    "models": [
      {
        "key": "vllm/qwen3-5-122b-a10b-nvfp4",
        "name": "qwen3-5-122b-a10b-nvfp4",
        "input": "text",
        "contextWindow": 200000,
        "local": false,
        "available": true,
        "tags": [
          "default",
          "configured",
          "alias:Qwen binary thinking"
        ],
        "missing": false
      }
    ]
  }

  $ OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-vllm-think-proof/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-vllm-think-proof/state pnpm openclaw infer model run --local --model vllm/qwen3-5-122b-a10b-nvfp4 --prompt 'Reply exactly: VLLM_THINK_OK' --thinking off --json
  {
    "ok": true,
    "capability": "model.run",
    "transport": "local",
    "provider": "vllm",
    "model": "qwen3-5-122b-a10b-nvfp4",
    "attempts": [],
    "outputs": [
      {
        "text": "VLLM_THINK_OK",
        "mediaUrl": null
      }
    ]
  }
  ```

- Observed result after fix: the isolated config's allowlist-only vLLM/Qwen model appears in the OpenClaw model list without leaking `params`, and a real OpenClaw local inference run reaches the live vLLM Qwen model with `--thinking off` and returns the requested output.
- What was not tested: a separate Nemotron live deployment was not available in this environment.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: live proof used the currently running local k3s vLLM Qwen deployment; request payload internals were validated by focused unit coverage rather than packet/log capture.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): review finding reproduced the gap where allowlist-only `agents.defaults.models["vllm/Qwen/Qwen3-8B"].params` did not populate the configured catalog used by thinking policy resolution.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=120000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/model-selection.test.ts --reporter verbose`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=120000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extensions.config.ts extensions/vllm/provider-policy-api.test.ts extensions/vllm/stream.test.ts extensions/vllm/provider-discovery.contract.test.ts --reporter verbose`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=120000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.auto-reply.config.ts src/auto-reply/thinking.test.ts --reporter verbose`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=120000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-methods.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/models.test.ts --reporter verbose`
- `pnpm lint --threads=8`
- `pnpm check:test-types`
- `pnpm run lint:extensions:bundled`
- `pnpm docs:list`
- `pnpm lint:docs`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm build`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- vLLM provider thinking policy tests for Qwen params/compat and Nemotron binary profiles.
- vLLM stream tests for params/compat/model params and Qwen patching when reasoning metadata is absent but not explicitly disabled.
- Model-selection tests for params merging, allowlist-only synthetic catalog entries, manifest-normalized provider metadata, and preserving discovered catalog names.
- Auto-reply thinking tests for passing catalog params/compat into provider policy hooks.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- PR CI initially failed `check-lint`, `check-test-types`, and bundled-extension lint; the branch now includes fixes for those local reproductions.

If no test was added, why not?

What is the highest-risk area?

How is that risk mitigated?

## Current review state

target.title: fix(codex): cap managed app-server trace logs
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes/ref: #86214

Adds startup retention for OpenClaw-managed Codex app-server trace logs under `CODEX_HOME`:

- applies before shared and isolated Codex app-server startup
- rotates `logs_2.sqlite`, `logs_2.sqlite-wal`, and `logs_2.sqlite-shm` when their combined size exceeds the cap
- defaults to a 512 MiB cap and keeps the 2 newest retired snapshots
- exposes env controls for disable/max-bytes/snapshot count
- logs a warning when rotation happens, but does not fail app-server startup if retention itself fails

This deliberately bounds OpenClaw-managed storage without needing to know the native Codex SQLite schema.

## Why

In one affected OpenClaw install, the managed Codex DB grew to ~43 GiB / 14.5M rows in under 8 days, while standalone Codex on the same machine had only an ~81 MiB `~/.codex/logs_2.sqlite`.

The live process chain was:

`openclaw gateway -> codex.js app-server -> native codex app-server --listen stdio://`

The growing file was:

`~/.openclaw/agents/main/agent/codex-home/logs_2.sqlite`

Additional evidence was posted on #86214:

https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/86214#issuecomment-4530473714

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** OpenClaw-managed Codex app-server log storage must not grow without a bounded retention path when `logs_2.sqlite` accumulates high-volume response/WebSocket/SSE trace rows.
- **Real environment tested:** Affected local macOS OpenClaw install on the same machine that produced the ~43 GiB `~/.openclaw/agents/main/agent/codex-home/logs_2.sqlite`; standalone Codex on the same machine was only ~81 MiB.
- **Exact steps or command run after fix:** Applied the local retention mitigation for the affected OpenClaw-managed Codex home, then captured the live retention state with `bin/openclaw-storage-retention --json --apply-safe` and `state/storage-retention/latest.json`. This PR upstreams the app-server startup cap so OpenClaw-managed Codex homes get a bounded version of the same protection before startup.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from the affected OpenClaw setup after the retention run:

```json
{
  "status": "ok",
  "attention_required": false,
  "before_size_gib": 0.733,
  "after_size_gib": 0.733,
  "remaining_old_rows": null,
  "deleted_rows": 2544,
  "compact_status": "skipped",
  "diagnosis_path": "/Users/.../.openclaw/workspace/state/storage-retention/codex-log-summary-20260524T225926Z.json"
}
```

Earlier in the same incident, pruning and VACUUM brought the OpenClaw-managed Codex DB from ~43 GiB / ~14.5M rows down to ~678 MiB with `remaining_old_rows=0`. The follow-up retention run above shows the same real OpenClaw setup stayed healthy and only had 2,544 additional expired rows to delete.

- **Observed result after fix:** The affected OpenClaw install no longer has old Codex log rows accumulating past the retention boundary, compaction was not needed on the follow-up run, and the watchdog reported `status: ok` / `attention_required: false`.
- **What was not tested:** This PR does not reduce native Codex trace volume by category, and I did not replace the production OpenClaw gateway binary with this branch on the Mac mini. The patch bounds OpenClaw-managed `CODEX_HOME` storage at app-server startup; native trace downsampling can be a separate follow-up.

## Config

- `OPENCLAW_CODEX_APP_SERVER_LOG_RETENTION=0|false|off|no` disables retention
- `OPENCLAW_CODEX_APP_SERVER_LOG_MAX_BYTES=<positive integer>` overrides the default cap
- `OPENCLAW_CODEX_APP_SERVER_RETIRED_LOG_SNAPSHOTS=<non-negative integer>` overrides retained retired snapshots

## Tests

- `pnpm exec oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/codex/src/app-server/log-retention.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/log-retention.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/shared-client.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test`
- `pnpm test:extension codex -- extensions/codex/src/app-server/log-retention.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/shared-client.test.ts`

## Follow-up

This does not reduce the native Codex app-server's trace volume by category. It prevents unbounded disk growth for OpenClaw-managed Codex homes. A separate follow-up could add native trace throttling or category-level filtering if maintainers want a smaller steady-state DB.

target.title: fix(agents): log warnings instead of swallowing subagent errors
target.body:
## Problem

Two catch blocks in the subagent registry silently discard errors, leaving no diagnostic trail when hook emission or disk restore fails:

1. **`emitSubagentEndedHookOnce`** (`subagent-registry-completion.ts:116`): `catch { return false; }` discards hook-runner errors when emitting the `subagent_ended` lifecycle hook.
2. **`restoreSubagentRunsOnce`** (`subagent-registry.ts:645`): `catch { // ignore restore failures }` discards errors when restoring subagent runs from disk on cold start.

When either path fails (hook runner unavailable, corrupt disk state, reconciliation error), the system continues silently. Operators have no indication that subagent lifecycle hooks were skipped or that restored runs were lost.

## Fix

Wire `log.warn()` into both catch blocks so errors are logged before the existing fallback behavior continues:

1. **Hook emission path**: `catch (err) { log.warn("failed to emit subagent_ended hook for run ${runId}: ..."); return false; }`
2. **Restore path**: `catch (err) { log.warn("failed to restore subagent runs from disk: ..."); }`

Both paths still return/continue as before (no behavior change beyond logging). The completion file gets its own `createSubsystemLogger("agents/subagent-registry-completion")` to match the existing `createSubsystemLogger("agents/subagent-registry")` in the registry file.

Production diff is +8 lines changed across 2 files.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Two silent `catch` blocks in the subagent registry discard errors during hook emission and disk restore. The fix adds `log.warn()` to both paths so failures leave a diagnostic trail instead of being silently swallowed.

Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2), Node.js v22.16.0, OpenClaw built from source (commit 1d5b5db) at `/tmp/fix-82943`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
Step 1. Build OpenClaw from patched source
cd /tmp/fix-82943
CI=true pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
pnpm build

Step 2. Verify both log.warn calls are in compiled production code
grep -n "failed to emit subagent_ended hook" dist/subagent-registry-DLVHJynj.js
grep -n "failed to restore subagent runs" dist/subagent-registry-DLVHJynj.js

Step 3. Start the patched gateway
timeout 10 node openclaw.mjs gateway

Step 4. Run test suites for both changed files
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-registry-completion.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

**Compiled production code verification**

Both `log.warn` calls are present in the compiled subagent-registry bundle:

```console
$ grep -n "failed to emit subagent_ended hook" dist/subagent-registry-DLVHJynj.js
94:log$2.warn(`failed to emit subagent_ended hook for run ${runId}: ${err instanceof Error ? err.message : String(err)}`);

$ grep -n "failed to restore subagent runs" dist/subagent-registry-DLVHJynj.js
1911:log.warn(`failed to restore subagent runs from disk: ${err instanceof Error ? err.message : String(err)}`);
```

Line 94 is `emitSubagentEndedHookOnce` (the hook emission path). Line 1911 is `restoreSubagentRunsOnce` (the disk restore path). Before this fix, both were `catch { }` (empty) and `catch { // ignore restore failures }` respectively.

The `log$2` prefix on line 94 is the compiled form of the new `createSubsystemLogger("agents/subagent-registry-completion")`, while `log` on line 1911 is the existing `createSubsystemLogger("agents/subagent-registry")`.

**Live gateway startup proof**

The patched gateway starts, loads the subagent registry with both warning-enabled catch blocks, and runs cleanly:

```console
$ timeout 10 node openclaw.mjs gateway
2026-05-21T12:39:28.862-07:00 [gateway] loading configuration…
2026-05-21T12:39:28.917-07:00 [gateway] starting...
2026-05-21T12:39:33.527-07:00 [health-monitor] started (interval: 300s, startup-grace: 60s, channel-connect-grace: 120s)
2026-05-21T12:39:34.421-07:00 [gateway] agent model: openai/gpt-5.5 (thinking=medium, fast=off)
2026-05-21T12:39:34.422-07:00 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins: acpx, browser, canvas, device-pair, file-transfer, memory-core, phone-control, talk-voice; 5.5s)
2026-05-21T12:39:34.913-07:00 [gateway] ready
2026-05-21T12:39:52.803-07:00 [gateway] signal SIGTERM received
2026-05-21T12:39:52.807-07:00 [gateway] received SIGTERM; shutting down
2026-05-21T12:39:52.856-07:00 [shutdown] completed cleanly in 38ms
```

No `[agents/subagent-registry-completion]` or `[agents/subagent-registry]` warnings appear in the startup log, confirming the catch blocks are not triggered during normal operation (they are defensive logging for error paths only).

**Vitest test suites (supplemental)**

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-registry-completion.test.ts
 ✓ |agents-core| subagent-registry-completion.test.ts (8 tests) 86ms
 ✓ |agents-support| subagent-registry-completion.test.ts (8 tests) 73ms
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  16 passed (16)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts
 ✓ |agents-core| subagent-registry.test.ts (29 tests) 2679ms
 ✓ |agents-support| subagent-registry.test.ts (29 tests) 2264ms
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  58 passed (58)
```

**Fault injection proof: restore catch path fires in real gateway**

The `restoreSubagentRunsOnce` catch handler was triggered in a **live running gateway** by injecting `throw new Error("INJECTED: corrupt registry state")` at the top of the try block in the compiled `dist/subagent-registry-DLVHJynj.js`. This forces the catch path to fire during gateway startup.

**Steps:**
1. Back up the compiled subagent-registry bundle.
2. Inject `throw new Error(...)` inside the try block of `restoreSubagentRunsOnce` (after `restoreAttempted = true`, before `restoreSubagentRunsFromDisk`).
3. Start the gateway.
4. Observe the `log.warn` message in the gateway output.
5. Confirm the gateway continues to start normally (the catch swallows the error).
6. Restore the original bundle.

**Terminal output (fault injection):**

```console
2026-05-21T16:15:54.095-07:00 [gateway] starting...
2026-05-21T16:15:54.710-07:00 [agents/subagent-registry] failed to restore subagent runs from disk: INJECTED: corrupt registry state
2026-05-21T16:15:54.832-07:00 [gateway] starting HTTP server...
2026-05-21T16:15:56.662-07:00 [gateway] agent model: openai/gpt-5.5 (thinking=medium, fast=off)
2026-05-21T16:15:56.665-07:00 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins; 2.6s)
2026-05-21T16:15:57.476-07:00 [gateway] ready
```

**Line-by-line analysis:**

- `[gateway] starting...`: Gateway startup begins.
- `[agents/subagent-registry] failed to restore subagent runs from disk: INJECTED: corrupt registry state`: The **`log.warn` in the catch handler fired**. The error message includes the full error text. Before this fix, this catch block was `catch { // ignore restore failures }` with no logging at all.
- `[gateway] ready`: The gateway **continued to start normally** despite the restore failure. The catch handler swallowed the error and let startup proceed.

Without this fix, the same error would be silently ignored (empty catch block with only a comment), leaving no diagnostic trail.

**Fault injection proof: hook emission catch path fires in real gateway**

The `emitSubagentEndedHookOnce` catch handler was also triggered in a **live running gateway**. A conditional `throw` was injected inside the try block of `emitSubagentEndedHookOnce` in `dist/subagent-registry-DLVHJynj.js` (line 73), and a timed trigger called the function after the gateway was ready.

**Terminal output (hook emission catch):**

```console
2026-05-21T21:35:44.608-07:00 [gateway] ready
2026-05-21T21:35:58.683-07:00 [agents/subagent-registry-completion] failed to emit subagent_ended hook for run INJECTED-PROOF-RUN: INJECTED: hook runner unavailable
2026-05-21T21:35:58.686-07:00 [gateway] signal SIGTERM received
2026-05-21T21:35:58.734-07:00 [gmail-watcher] gmail watcher stopped
2026-05-21T21:35:58.736-07:00 [shutdown] completed cleanly in 38ms
```

**Analysis:**
- `[agents/subagent-registry-completion]` subsystem prefix confirms the **new** `createSubsystemLogger("agents/subagent-registry-completion")` is active (this logger was added by this PR).
- `failed to emit subagent_ended hook for run INJECTED-PROOF-RUN: INJECTED: hook runner unavailable` is the exact `log.warn` message from the catch block at compiled line 94.
- The gateway continued running after the hook emission failure (the catch returns `false`, not rethrow).
- Before this fix, this catch block was `catch { return false; }` with no logging at all.

**Both catch blocks are now proven in live gateway runs:**
1. **Restore catch** (`restoreSubagentRunsOnce`): `[agents/subagent-registry] failed to restore subagent runs from disk: INJECTED: corrupt registry state`
2. **Hook emission catch** (`emitSubagentEndedHookOnce`): `[agents/subagent-registry-completion] failed to emit subagent_ended hook for run INJECTED-PROOF-RUN: INJECTED: hook runner unavailable`

Observed result after fix: Both new `log.warn` calls fire correctly in production compiled code. The restore catch logs under `[agents/subagent-registry]` and the hook emission catch logs under `[agents/subagent-registry-completion]`. Both allow the gateway to continue running. All 74 tests pass across both test files.

What was not tested: The hook emission was triggered via injected timer call rather than a real subagent completion event. The injection proves the catch handler mechanism (log.warn + return false + gateway continues) works identically to the restore path proof.

target.title: fix(ui): reset routed sessions to main on restore
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: Control UI could restore a historical routed session key like `agent:*:*:(direct|group):*` from persisted local settings after reload.
- Why it matters: users would reopen Control UI and land in a stale routed session instead of the real main session, making the UI look misrouted even though the backend heartbeat target remained main.
- What changed: session restore now sanitizes persisted routed direct/group session keys during settings load and falls back to `main` instead.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no gateway routing behavior, heartbeat behavior, or live session switching logic was changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #61661
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Control UI restoring historical routed direct/group sessions from persisted local settings instead of falling back to main.
- Real environment tested: local checkout of `openclaw/openclaw` on Linux.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `pnpm vitest run ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
  - `pnpm tsgo:core`
  - `pnpm lint:core`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
```text
✓ ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts ... falls back to main when restoring a routed direct session
✓ ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts ... falls back to main when restoring a routed group session
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       18 passed (18)
```
- Observed result after fix: persisted `agent:*:*:(direct|group):*` session selections are normalized back to `main` during settings restore.
- What was not tested: explicit session selection through URL query params or live backend routing changes, because this PR only changes local restore behavior.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Control UI storage restore trusted persisted `sessionKey` / `lastActiveSessionKey` values without filtering out historical routed direct/group sessions.
- Missing detection / guardrail: storage tests covered scoped session persistence but did not cover stale routed-session restore behavior.
- Contributing context (if known): main-session normalization already existed later in the gateway boot path, but the bad routed session had already won at the storage restore layer.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: persisted routed `direct`/`group` session keys fall back to `main` on Control UI restore.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug originates in local persisted settings load, so the storage tests are the exact boundary where the regression occurs.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): branch adds two restore-specific cases for routed `direct` and `group` sessions.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

After reload/reopen, Control UI will no longer restore stale routed `direct`/`group` sessions from persisted local settings; it will fall back to the main session instead.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Area: Control UI local storage restore

### Steps

1. Seed persisted Control UI settings with a routed direct/group session key in `sessionsByGateway`.
2. Call `loadSettings()`.
3. Verify restored `sessionKey` / `lastActiveSessionKey` fall back to `main`.

### Expected

- routed historical sessions should not win restore by default
- restored settings should point back to main

### Actual

- both new routed-session storage tests pass
- `tsgo:core` passes
- `lint:core` passes

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified the restore sanitization happens in `ui/src/ui/storage.ts`, which is the correct layer for this bug.
- Verified routed `direct` and `group` session keys now restore to `main` in storage tests.
- Verified core type/lint checks pass on the changed files.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a user might intentionally want to reopen a routed direct/group session from persisted state.
  - Mitigation: this PR only changes the automatic restore default; explicit session selection can still be handled through current UI/navigation flows.
- Risk: over-matching could reset legitimate session keys.
  - Mitigation: the sanitization is narrowly limited to `agent:*:*:(direct|group):*` patterns only.



---

## Real behavior proof

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.5 /tmp/pincer-openclaw

 ✓  ui  ../../ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts (22 tests) 38ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  22 passed (22)
    Start at  00:14:43
    Duration  611ms
```

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Routed direct/group session keys persisted in localStorage were not cleared on restore. The regex `/^agent:[^:]+:[^:]+:(direct|group):.+$/` only matched 5-segment per-channel-peer keys, missing `agent:<id>:direct:<peer>` (per-peer) and `agent:<id>:<channel>:<acct>:direct:<peer>` (per-account-channel-peer) shapes emitted by `buildAgentPeerSessionKey`.
**Real environment tested:** Node.js v24.14.0, vitest v4.1.5, jsdom, Linux.
**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts`
**After-fix evidence:** 22/22 tests pass including two new tests covering per-peer (`agent:main:direct:ou_123`) and per-account-channel-peer (`agent:main:feishu:acct_abc:direct:ou_123`) shapes, both correctly falling back to `main`.
**Observed result after fix:** All three `buildAgentPeerSessionKey` direct shapes and group shapes reset to main on storage restore.
**What was not tested:** Live Control UI reload in a browser with actual localStorage entries; all three direct key shapes verified via jsdom storage unit tests.

## Current-head follow-up proof — 2026-05-20

Behavior or issue addressed:
- Covers ClawSweeper's threaded per-peer routed session gap for agent:<id>:direct:<peer>:thread:<id> and legacy dm equivalents.

Real environment tested:
- PR branch fix/control-ui-routed-session-restore at commit 156d49e17c.
- Local Control UI storage module executed through jsdom with real loadSettings() and browser-like localStorage/sessionStorage.
- Full Chromium proof was attempted, but this container's headless Chromium cannot launch because libglib-2.0.so.0 is unavailable; the prior connected-browser proof still covers the Control UI reload path for the earlier provider-scoped routed shape.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
~~~text
COREPACK_HOME=/home/node/.openclaw/workspace/agents/pr-machine/workspace/.corepack pnpm -s vitest run ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts
CI=true COREPACK_HOME=/home/node/.openclaw/workspace/agents/pr-machine/workspace/.corepack pnpm -s check:test-types
git diff --check
node --import tsx <jsdom loadSettings proof script>
~~~

Evidence after fix:
~~~text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  33 passed (33)

tsgo core/extensions test completed successfully
git diff --check completed successfully

{"currentHead":"156d49e17c","seededSession":"agent:main:direct:ou_123:thread:99","loadedGatewayUrl":"wss://gateway.example:8443","restoredSessionKey":"main","restoredLastActiveSessionKey":"main","restoredToMain":true}
{"currentHead":"156d49e17c","seededSession":"agent:main:dm:12345:thread:12345:99","loadedGatewayUrl":"wss://gateway.example:8443","restoredSessionKey":"main","restoredLastActiveSessionKey":"main","restoredToMain":true}
~~~

Observed result after fix:
- Threaded per-peer direct and legacy dm routed session keys now restore to main for both sessionKey and lastActiveSessionKey.

What was not tested:
- A fresh current-head Chromium screenshot was not captured because Playwright's bundled Chromium cannot start in this container due the missing libglib-2.0.so.0 system library.

target.title: fix(agents): strip plaintext model provider keys
target.body:
## Summary
- strip plaintext provider `apiKey` values after merge-mode preservation, source-managed enforcement, and provider compatibility transforms so old `models.json` keys cannot be reintroduced into the final prompt-facing catalog
- clean legacy catalog-only `models.json` plaintext provider keys before the empty-provider `skip` path can preserve them
- migrate existing `models.json`-only plaintext provider keys into the target agent's local `auth-profiles.json` before writing the stripped catalog when there is no configured provider key and no target-local auth profile for that provider
- keep inherited main-agent profiles and runtime/external overlays from suppressing local legacy `models.json` key migration for non-main agents
- migrate known/env-present legacy env-name catalog credentials such as `LITELLM_KEY` to env-backed auth-profile `keyRef`s, while preserving ambiguous env-shaped plaintext strings as inline local auth-profile keys
- preserve non-secret markers such as env SecretRef markers and custom-provider `secretref-managed` compatibility markers
- add regression coverage for generated plaintext stripping, merge-mode stripping, catalog-only cleanup, existing-key migration, fail-closed behavior, inherited-profile migration boundaries, env-present keyRef migration, ambiguous env-shaped key preservation, and pi model discovery compatibility

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Generated and legacy `models.json` files should not persist plaintext provider `apiKey` values. Cleanup should preserve upgrade auth outside the prompt-facing catalog, including non-main agent local migration boundaries and env-shaped legacy values that must only become `keyRef`s when an env marker or observed env value proves they are env-backed.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source from this PR branch at `b23d1d7c`, macOS, Node.js `v24.1.0`, `/usr/bin/python3` for fs-safe pinned writes. Provider keys in proof output are redacted.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
FS_SAFE_PYTHON_MODE=require FS_SAFE_PYTHON=/usr/bin/python3 node --import tsx --input-type=module
```

The inline Node proof script created temporary local agent dirs, called `ensureOpenClawModelsJson`, `discoverModels`, `ensureAuthProfileStore`, and `createProviderAuthResolver`, and exercised these real paths: inline custom provider stripping, legacy configured `models.json` key migration, catalog-only cleanup, env-present `keyRef` migration, ambiguous env-shaped plaintext preservation, non-main inherited-profile migration boundary, and pi model discovery compatibility.

- Evidence after fix (copied live output, redacted):

```json
{
  "head": "b23d1d7c",
  "tempRoot": "<tmp>",
  "inlineCustomProvider": {
    "apiKeyMarker": "secretref-managed",
    "hasPlaintextInModelsJson": false,
    "discoverModelsFindsCustomModel": "custom-model"
  },
  "legacyModelsJsonOnlyUpgrade": {
    "apiKeyMarker": "secretref-managed",
    "hasPlaintextInModelsJson": false,
    "migratedProfile": {
      "type": "api_key",
      "provider": "custom",
      "copyToAgents": false,
      "displayName": "Migrated from models.json",
      "key": "<redacted>"
    },
    "discoverModelsFindsLegacyModel": "legacy-model"
  },
  "catalogOnlyLegacyCleanup": {
    "apiKeyMarker": "secretref-managed",
    "hasPlaintextInModelsJson": false,
    "migratedProfile": {
      "type": "api_key",
      "provider": "custom",
      "copyToAgents": false,
      "displayName": "Migrated from models.json",
      "key": "<redacted>"
    },
    "discoverModelsFindsCatalogOnlyModel": "catalog-only-model"
  },
  "envPresentCatalogMigration": {
    "modelsJsonPromptSafe": true,
    "migratedProfile": {
      "type": "api_key",
      "provider": "custom",
      "hasInlineKey": false,
      "keyRef": {
        "source": "env",
        "provider": "default",
        "id": "LITELLM_KEY"
      },
      "copyToAgents": false
    },
    "runtimeAuth": {
      "mode": "api_key",
      "source": "profile",
      "profileId": "custom:models-json",
      "apiKeyMarker": "LITELLM_KEY",
      "resolvedEnvValueSeen": true
    }
  },
  "ambiguousEnvShapedCredential": {
    "modelsJsonPromptSafe": true,
    "migratedProfile": {
      "type": "api_key",
      "provider": "custom",
      "hasInlineKey": true,
      "hasKeyRef": false,
      "copyToAgents": false
    },
    "runtimeAuth": {
      "mode": "api_key",
      "source": "profile",
      "profileId": "custom:models-json",
      "preservedLocalKeySeen": true
    }
  },
  "inheritedProfileBoundary": {
    "modelsJsonPromptSafe": true,
    "localMigratedProfile": {
      "type": "api_key",
      "provider": "custom",
      "copyToAgents": false,
      "displayName": "Migrated from models.json",
      "key": "<redacted>"
    },
    "runtimeAuth": {
      "mode": "api_key",
      "source": "profile",
      "profileId": "custom:models-json",
      "resolvedWorkerKey": true
    }
  }
}
```

- Observed result after fix: the final `models.json` omits plaintext keys; non-built-in custom providers with inline model rows retain the non-secret `secretref-managed` marker; old configured and catalog-only `models.json` credentials migrate into `auth-profiles.json`; env-present legacy catalog values migrate as env-backed `keyRef` credentials and resolve through provider auth; ambiguous env-shaped legacy values with no env signal are preserved as inline local auth-profile keys instead of unresolved env refs; a non-main agent with an inherited same-profile ID still migrates its local legacy catalog key and runtime auth resolves the worker-local key; and pi `discoverModels` can still find custom model rows.
- What was not tested: I did not test a real paid third-party provider. The live proof did not force auth-profile persistence failure; that fail-closed path is covered by the focused regression test listed below.

target.title: Guard OpenAI chat payload turns
target.body:
Fixes #86145

## Summary
- Refuse OpenAI-compatible chat streams when compaction/transport transforms leave a system/tool-only payload with no user or assistant turns.
- Classify blank prompt submissions with system/tool-only replay as empty history instead of replay-backed blank user prompts.
- Add regressions for the stream-time guard and compaction branch recovery of user/assistant turns when preparation only contains tool output.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Qwen/OpenAI-compatible chat requests no longer reach the provider with a `messages[]` payload that has no user or assistant turn after compaction and transport transforms.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw runtime on this PR branch, using `createOpenAICompletionsTransportStreamFn` against a real local HTTP/SSE endpoint that behaves like an OpenAI-compatible `/v1/chat/completions` server.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node --import tsx --input-type=module` with a script that first sends a positive-control user turn to the loopback OpenAI-compatible endpoint, then sends a system-only runtime prompt through the same stream function.

Evidence after fix: Redacted terminal output from the runtime proof run:

```json
{
  "proof": "PR #86177 OpenAI-compatible runtime guard",
  "runtime": "real local OpenAI-compatible HTTP/SSE endpoint via createOpenAICompletionsTransportStreamFn",
  "model": "mlx/mlx-community/Qwen3-30B-A3B-6bit",
  "endpoint": "http://127.0.0.1:[REDACTED_PORT]/v1",
  "positive_control": {
    "requests_received": 1,
    "captured_roles": ["system", "user"],
    "done_reason": "stop"
  },
  "system_only_guard": {
    "requests_before": 1,
    "requests_after": 1,
    "network_requests_delta": 0,
    "error_event_seen": true,
    "stop_reason": "error",
    "error_message": "OpenAI-compatible chat payload for mlx/mlx-community/Qwen3-30B-A3B-6bit contains no user or assistant messages after compaction and transport transforms; refusing to send a system/tool-only request. Start a new user turn or repair the compacted session history."
  },
  "result": "PASS: system-only OpenAI-compatible payload was caught locally before HTTP send"
}
```

Observed result after fix: The positive-control request reached the loopback endpoint and captured `messages` roles `["system", "user"]`, proving the runtime endpoint was live and normal OpenAI-compatible sends still reach the network. The system-only run emitted the new local error, and the loopback endpoint request count stayed unchanged (`network_requests_delta: 0`), showing the malformed payload was blocked locally before `chat.completions.create` sent an HTTP request to the endpoint.

What was not tested: No external paid or remote Qwen provider call was made. This proof uses a real local OpenAI-compatible HTTP/SSE endpoint to show the transport guard fires before network send; maintainers should still decide whether the user/assistant-free payload invariant should remain global or be provider-compat scoped.

## Validation
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.prompt-helpers.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts`

- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.prompt-helpers.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.prompt-helpers.test.ts src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

If this PR is squashed or reworked, please preserve Andy Ye as the author or include:

target.title: fix: recover Codex binding after stale preflight compaction
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #86211.

This PR makes the Telegram/Codex preflight compaction path recover from stale native harness thread bindings instead of aborting inbound dispatch before an assistant run can start.

Changes:
- treats structured stale_thread_binding / missing_thread_binding native harness compaction failures as recoverable during preflight compaction
- keeps non-binding compaction failures fail-closed and visible
- lets Codex binding recovery create a fresh app-server thread when the old binding has already been cleared
- adds focused regression coverage for both the preflight dispatch path and the cleared-binding Codex recovery path

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Inbound Telegram/Codex dispatch no longer aborts when preflight compaction sees a stale or missing Codex app-server thread binding. The recoverable stale-thread or missing-thread result now returns the current session entry so dispatch can continue, and the binding layer creates a fresh app-server thread if the binding file has already been cleared.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout at /root/.openclaw/workspace/openclaw-upstream on Linux with Node 22.22.2, branch fix-86211-missing-thread-preflight, commit 4b8ff8685fd9d4d3bcd901c30abc76ce9c98dd34, rebased on current origin/main.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran targeted OpenClaw runtime checks in the local checkout: node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.auto-reply-reply.config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts; node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extensions.config.ts extensions/codex/src/conversation-binding.test.ts; node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extensions.config.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/compact.test.ts; node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-support.config.ts src/agents/command/cli-compaction.test.ts; focused npx oxlint on the changed core and extension files.

Evidence after fix: Local terminal output showed agent-runner-memory.test.ts passed with 27 tests, conversation-binding.test.ts passed with 13 tests, compact.test.ts passed with 26 tests, cli-compaction.test.ts passed with 11 tests, and focused oxlint exited 0 for the changed core and extension files. The command output was captured after rebasing on origin/main; PR text uses only redacted placeholders such as <codex-thread-id> and no chat IDs.

Observed result after fix: The stale-binding and missing-binding preflight regressions no longer throw "Preflight compaction required but failed: thread not found: <codex-thread-id>" or the equivalent missing-binding failure for structured stale_thread_binding or missing_thread_binding results. Non-binding failures still throw. The cleared-binding Codex conversation regression creates a fresh thread/start, runs turn/start on that new thread, returns a reply, and saves the new binding.

What was not tested: Live Telegram group delivery against a production gateway running this PR build was not tested. The proof uses the local OpenClaw checkout and targeted runtime paths with sanitized evidence. I requested the repository Mantis Telegram proof path in PR comment https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/86216#issuecomment-4530350483, but direct workflow dispatch requires maintainer/admin rights.

## Validation

Passed locally:
- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.auto-reply-reply.config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts
- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extensions.config.ts extensions/codex/src/conversation-binding.test.ts
- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extensions.config.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/compact.test.ts
- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-support.config.ts src/agents/command/cli-compaction.test.ts
- npx oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts
- npx oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/codex/src/conversation-binding.ts extensions/codex/src/conversation-binding.test.ts

Also ran node scripts/check-changed.mjs. Its typecheck/core lint/guard steps passed; the broad extension lint subprocess was killed locally after several minutes, so I followed with focused extension oxlint on the changed files, which passed.

## OpenClaw proof gate status

The repository OpenClaw `Real behavior proof` workflow has now re-evaluated the current PR head and passed.

- Head: `4b8ff8685fd9d4d3bcd901c30abc76ce9c98dd34`
- Run: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/26375423624
- Job log result: `External PR includes after-fix real behavior proof.`

This means the remaining status is no longer a contributor-side Mantis dispatch blocker. The earlier Mantis request is kept only as historical context for the previous proof suggestion; it is not the active unblock path.

A reviewer can now re-check the current PR state against the passing OpenClaw proof gate and the focused validation above.

## Privacy

All issue and PR evidence is sanitized. Production chat IDs, message IDs, and Codex thread IDs are redacted or replaced with placeholders.

target.title: fix(install): validate downloaded scripts before execution
target.body:
## Problem

Four code paths in `scripts/install.sh` download remote scripts via `download_file` and execute them with `bash "$tmp"` without any validation. While HTTPS/TLS1.2 protects the transport, truncated downloads, HTML error pages (e.g., CDN 403 responses), and empty responses would be silently executed as bash scripts.

Affected paths:
1. `run_remote_bash` (Homebrew installer from `raw.githubusercontent.com`)
2. NodeSource apt/deb setup script (`deb.nodesource.com/setup_*.x`)
3. NodeSource dnf/rpm setup script (`rpm.nodesource.com/setup_*.x`)
4. NodeSource yum/rpm setup script (`rpm.nodesource.com/setup_*.x`)

Contrast with `bootstrap_gum_temp` which verifies downloaded content with SHA256 checksums.

## Fix

Extract a reusable `validate_downloaded_script()` function and apply it to all four download-then-execute paths. The validation:
1. Rejects empty files (`[[ ! -s "$file" ]]`)
2. Rejects files that don't start with a shebang (`#!`)
3. Sanitizes the first-line diagnostic before logging: strips control characters (bytes 0x00-0x1F and 0x7F) via `LC_ALL=C tr -d '\000-\037\177'` and escapes backslashes (`${safe_line//\\/\\\\}`) so untrusted downloaded content cannot inject terminal escape sequences through `ui_error`'s `echo -e` path.

`run_remote_bash` calls `validate_downloaded_script` internally. The three NodeSource paths call it explicitly after `download_file` and before `bash "$tmp"` (or `sudo bash "$tmp"`).

A full SHA256 checksum pin is impractical because these setup scripts update frequently and a pinned hash would break the installer on every release.

**Sanitization detail:** The `ui_error` function uses `echo -e` in the non-gum fallback path, which interprets backslash sequences (`\033[...` ANSI escapes, `\n` newlines, etc.). Two layers prevent terminal injection: (1) `tr -d` strips raw control bytes (actual ESC 0x1B), (2) backslash escaping prevents `echo -e` from interpreting literal `\033[31m` text as ANSI escapes.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `run_remote_bash()` executed downloaded content with `/bin/bash` unconditionally. Truncated downloads, HTML error pages, JSON errors, and binary blobs would all be piped to bash. Additionally, the first-line diagnostic in the rejection message passed untrusted content through `ui_error`'s `echo -e` path, which interprets backslash escape sequences (terminal injection).

Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2, bash 5.x). The real patched `run_remote_bash` function was extracted from `scripts/install.sh` via `sed` and exercised against a local Python HTTP server serving 4 response types.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
Step 1 -- start a local HTTP server serving test responses (HTML error, empty, valid script, escape attack). Step 2 -- extract the real `run_remote_bash` from the patched `install.sh` and call it against each endpoint:
```bash
python3 -c '<HTTP server serving /html-error, /empty, /valid-script, /escape-attack>'
eval "$(sed -n '/^run_remote_bash()/,/^}/p' scripts/install.sh)"
run_remote_bash "http://127.0.0.1:19877/html-error"
run_remote_bash "http://127.0.0.1:19877/empty"
run_remote_bash "http://127.0.0.1:19877/valid-script"
run_remote_bash "http://127.0.0.1:19877/escape-attack"
```

Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

**Live proof: real `run_remote_bash` against HTTP server**

The actual `run_remote_bash` function from the patched `install.sh` was extracted via `sed -n '/^run_remote_bash()/,/^}/p'` and called against a local Python HTTP server. This exercises the real patched code path, not a test harness copy.

```console
=== REAL run_remote_bash from patched install.sh ===

--- Test 1: HTML error page ---
✗ Downloaded file does not look like a shell script (no shebang): http://127.0.0.1:19877/html-error
✗ First line: <html><body>404 Not Found</body></html>
Exit code: 1

--- Test 2: Empty response ---
✗ Downloaded script is empty: http://127.0.0.1:19877/empty
Exit code: 1

--- Test 3: Valid #!/bin/bash script ---
hello-from-valid-script
Exit code: 0

--- Test 4: Terminal escape attack ---
✗ Downloaded file does not look like a shell script (no shebang): http://127.0.0.1:19877/escape-attack
✗ First line: \033[31mHACKED\033[0m terminal injection
Exit code: 1
```

**Test 1 (HTML):** Server returns `<html><body>404 Not Found</body></html>`. Rejected because no `#!` shebang. First line shown in diagnostic.

**Test 2 (empty):** Server returns empty body. Rejected by the `[[ ! -s "$tmp" ]]` check.

**Test 3 (valid script):** Server returns `#!/bin/bash\necho hello-from-valid-script`. Accepted, executed by `/bin/bash`, printed `hello-from-valid-script`.

**Test 4 (escape attack):** Server returns literal text `\033[31mHACKED\033[0m terminal injection` (ASCII backslash-zero-three-three, NOT actual ESC bytes). Rejected (no shebang). The first-line diagnostic shows `\033[31mHACKED\033[0m` as **literal text**, NOT as red-colored terminal output. Without the sanitization fix, `echo -e` would interpret `\033[31m` as ANSI red and show colored "HACKED" text. The double sanitization (control byte stripping via `tr -d` + backslash escaping via `${safe_line//\\/\\\\}`) prevents both attack vectors.

**Sanitization detail**

Two layers protect the first-line diagnostic from terminal injection:

1. **Control byte stripping** (`LC_ALL=C tr -d '\000-\037\177'`): Removes actual ESC (0x1B), NUL, and other control bytes from the downloaded content.
2. **Backslash escaping** (`${safe_line//\\/\\\\}`): Doubles all backslashes so `echo -e` treats `\033` as literal text instead of interpreting it as an ESC byte.

Verification with a crafted payload:
```console
Literal \033[31m text (7 ASCII chars, no real ESC byte):
$ echo -e "First line: \033[31mHACKED\033[0m"
First line: HACKED    # <-- RED colored text (dangerous!)

$ safe_line='\033[31mHACKED\033[0m'; safe_line="${safe_line//\\/\\\\}"
$ echo -e "First line: ${safe_line}"
First line: \033[31mHACKED\033[0m    # <-- plain text (safe!)
```

**Supplemental: validation harness (10/10 pass)**

```console
$ bash scripts/test-install-validation.sh
── GREEN: validation rejects bad downloads ──
  ✓ PASS  empty file  → rejected
  ✓ PASS  HTML error   → rejected
  ✓ PASS  JSON error   → rejected
  ✓ PASS  binary blob  → rejected
  ✓ PASS  valid script (#!bash)     → accepted
  ✓ PASS  valid script (#!env bash) → accepted

── RED: no validation lets bad downloads through ──
  ✓ PASS  empty file  → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  HTML error   → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  JSON error   → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  binary blob  → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)

Results: 10 passed, 0 failed
```

**Live HTTPS proof: unmodified `download_file` over TLS**

Proof using the **real unmodified `download_file` function** from `install.sh` (not a test stub). The function uses `curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 --retry 3` -- the identical flags used in production for the Homebrew installer download from `raw.githubusercontent.com`.

The server is a local Python `ssl.SSLContext` HTTPS server with a self-signed certificate (SAN: `IP:127.0.0.1`). `CURL_CA_BUNDLE` is set to trust the self-signed cert, so curl's TLS verification passes. No `--insecure` flag is used.

```console
=== REAL download_file + run_remote_bash over HTTPS ===
download_file: curl --proto =https --tlsv1.2 (unmodified from install.sh)
CURL_CA_BUNDLE=/tmp/test-cert.pem (trust self-signed cert for 127.0.0.1)

--- Test 1: HTTPS HTML error (real download_file) ---
x Downloaded file does not look like a shell script (no shebang): https://127.0.0.1:19879/html-error
x First line: <html><body>403 Forbidden</body></html>
Exit code: 1

--- Test 2: HTTPS empty (real download_file) ---
x Downloaded script is empty: https://127.0.0.1:19879/empty
Exit code: 1

--- Test 3: HTTPS valid script (real download_file) ---
hello-from-REAL-download-file-HTTPS
Exit code: 0
```

All three tests use the **unmodified `download_file` function** extracted via `sed` from `install.sh`. The function calls `curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 --retry 3 --retry-delay 1 --retry-connrefused` -- the identical command line used in production. No `--insecure` flag. `CURL_CA_BUNDLE` trusts the self-signed cert so TLS verification passes normally.

Observed result after fix: The real patched `run_remote_bash` and `validate_downloaded_script` functions reject HTML errors, empty responses, and escape-attack payloads while accepting valid `#!/bin/bash` scripts. The first-line diagnostic is sanitized against both raw control bytes and literal backslash escape sequences. The HTTPS proof uses the real unmodified `download_file` function with `--proto '=https' --tlsv1.2` over TLS against a self-signed cert trusted via `CURL_CA_BUNDLE`. All 10 validation harness tests pass.

What was not tested: Live download from `raw.githubusercontent.com` returning an actual CDN error page (structural validation is tested against local HTTP and HTTPS servers instead).





---

## ClawSweeper review findings addressed (a898dce97d)

### [P2] Raw byte shebang check (scripts/install.sh)

**Before:** `first_line` was populated via command substitution (`head -c 256 | head -1`), so bash discarded NUL bytes before the `#!` prefix check. A file with leading NUL bytes before `#!` would false-accept.

**After:** Uses `od -An -tx1 -N2` to read the first two raw file bytes and compare against `2321` (hex for `#!`). The command-substitution path is now only used for the diagnostic message on rejection.

NUL-prefix bypass proof:
```console
$ printf '\x00\x00#!/bin/bash\necho pwned\n' > /tmp/nul_test.sh
$ od -An -tx1 -N2 /tmp/nul_test.sh | tr -d ' '
0000
# Raw bytes are 0x00 0x00, not 0x23 0x21 → rejected
```

### [P3] Portable control-byte assertion (scripts/test-install-validation.sh)

**Before:** `grep -P '[\x00-\x1f\x7f\x80-\x9f]'` (GNU-only, fails on macOS BSD grep).

**After:** `tr -d '\000-\037\177\200-\237'` + string comparison. Fully POSIX-portable.

### Updated harness output (13/13 pass, macOS)

```console
$ bash scripts/test-install-validation.sh
── GREEN: validation rejects bad downloads ──
  ✓ PASS  empty file  → rejected
  ✓ PASS  HTML error   → rejected
  ✓ PASS  JSON error   → rejected
  ✓ PASS  binary blob  → rejected
  ✓ PASS  C1 escape    → rejected
  ✓ PASS  NUL prefix   → rejected (raw byte check caught NUL before #!)
  ✓ PASS  C1 diagnostic → C1 bytes stripped from error output
  ✓ PASS  valid script (#!bash)     → accepted
  ✓ PASS  valid script (#!env bash) → accepted

── RED: no validation lets bad downloads through ──
  ✓ PASS  empty file  → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  HTML error   → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  JSON error   → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  binary blob  → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)

Results: 13 passed, 0 failed
All checks passed — validation works as intended.
```

target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 4 constrained provider capabilities
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR keeps WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and BlueBubbles honest by making hosted, device-bound, self-hosted, Mac-hosted, and bridge-backed constraints visible instead of pretending they are equivalent official bot APIs. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, and prefix-ownership contract; carries a test-only upstream CI mock refresh. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, preview/progress gates, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app platform aliases, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 4 - Constrained/device-bound provider capabilities - Role: Adds constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers. - Head: `a9ee04cded` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `a9ee04cded`. Focused local validation after the latest stack hardening and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-runtime-api-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-support.config.ts src/agents/model-fallback.probe.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner.sanitize-session-history.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner.sanitize-session-history.policy.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner.openai-tool-id-preservation.test.ts src/agents/runtime-plan/build.test.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-runtime-api-guardrails.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts` - `pnpm exec oxlint --tsconfig extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json extensions/channel-broker/runtime-api.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/accounts.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/outbound.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/platforms.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts` - `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test` - `pnpm format:docs:check` - `pnpm lint:docs` - `pnpm docs:check-mdx` - `pnpm docs:check-links` - `pnpm docs:check-i18n-glossary` skipped locally because this worktree had no merge-base context; GitHub Actions remains the broad docs authority. - `git diff --check` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds Phase 4 constrained/device-bound provider metadata to the Channel Broker contract. - Covers WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and BlueBubbles-backed iMessage bridges as broker provider capabilities, not native plugin rewrites. - Stacks on Phase 3 official/app capability matrix (#86164), Phase 2D Slack proof (#86157), Phase 2C Discord proof (#86156), Phase 2B Telegram proof (#86154), Phase 2A conformance baseline (#86153), and Phase 1 broker foundation (#86096). Closes #86114 Related #86105 Related #86108 Supersedes closed PR #86148, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. ## What changed - Adds `constraints` and `badges` to broker platform/provider capabilities. - Models constrained provider facts such as: - `businessApi`, `cloudApi`, `providerHosted` - `deviceBound`, `linkedDevice`, `qrPairing`, `sessionFragile` - `selfHosted`, `phoneNumberRequired`, `signalCli` - `macHostRequired`, `messagesSignedIn`, `privateApiOptional`, `privateApiRequired` - `externalBridge` - Exposes resolved provider capabilities in channel-broker status snapshots so UI/status can show constrained-provider badges. - Adds WhatsApp, Signal, and iMessage target proof through `channel-broker`. - Documents constrained provider expectations in Channel Broker, SDK, WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and BlueBubbles migration docs. Focused diff for this stack layer: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Constrained providers expose hosted, device-bound, self-hosted, Mac-hosted, and bridge-backed limits through broker `constraints`/`badges`, and WhatsApp, Signal, and iMessage still route through provider-owned broker requests. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that evaluated constrained-provider capability support and called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` for `broker:whatsapp:direct:15551234567`, `broker:signal:direct:+15551234567`, and `broker:imessage:group:chat-guid-1?threadId=imessage-thread-1`. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "constrainedProviders": { "whatsappBusiness": true, "whatsappDeviceBound": false, "signalDeviceBound": true }, "outbound": [ { "label": "whatsapp-direct", "platform": "whatsapp", "conversation": { "id": "15551234567", "type": "direct" } }, { "label": "signal-direct", "platform": "signal", "conversation": { "id": "+15551234567", "type": "direct" } }, { "label": "imessage-group", "platform": "imessage", "conversation": { "id": "chat-guid-1", "type": "group", "threadId": "imessage-thread-1" } } ] } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live SDK/plugin path distinguished WhatsApp Business-style support from device-bound WhatsApp requirements, accepted Signal's self-hosted/device-bound constraint set, and produced broker provider requests for WhatsApp, Signal, and iMessage. - **What was not tested:** Live WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, or BlueBubbles-backed provider traffic; this PR proves the broker contract/schema/docs layer and leaves broad validation to GitHub Actions. ## Validation Run locally from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/channel-broker-vitest-include.json pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - `pnpm tsgo:core:test` - `git diff --check` - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/accounts.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/platforms.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/status.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/types.ts docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md docs/channels/whatsapp.md docs/channels/signal.md docs/channels/imessage.md docs/channels/imessage-from-bluebubbles.md` - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - `node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md docs/channels/whatsapp.md docs/channels/signal.md docs/channels/imessage.md docs/channels/imessage-from-bluebubbles.md` - `pnpm dlx --config.resolution-mode=highest markdownlint-cli2 --config config/markdownlint-cli2.jsonc docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md docs/channels/whatsapp.md docs/channels/signal.md docs/channels/imessage.md docs/channels/imessage-from-bluebubbles.md` ## Review state - Live for maintainer review; no longer draft. - This PR does not implement Phase 5 legacy-plugin freeze. - Next phase: #86115 freezes new legacy native-channel feature work after broker migration paths exist. This PR supplies the constrained-provider metadata needed before that freeze can be credible.

## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR prevents the long tail from becoming a new pile of native branches. It expresses official app/API platforms as broker aliases and capability rows first, and it now enforces declared delivery capabilities before outbound handoff. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, and prefix-ownership contract; carries a test-only upstream CI mock refresh. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, preview/progress gates, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app platform aliases, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 3 - Official/app platform capability matrix - Role: Adds official/app platform aliases, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. - Head: `1ca3f60b2f` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `1ca3f60b2f`. Focused local validation after the latest stack hardening and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` -
